results.document_preview: **Exhibit 10.14** **NSB SOFTWARE AS A SERVICE MASTER AGREEMENT** THIS AGREEMENT

attempts.claim: Because Section 3.5 requires Client to cease all use of the Software and destroy all copies upon termination, and Section 4.1 states NSB has no ownership rights in Client’s data, the Agreement obligates NSB to return all Client data to Client upon any termination of the Agreement.

Under the Agreement's definition of 'Software as a Service Fees,' those fees expressly include hardware lease and maintenance services and implementation services in addition to the rights to use the Software and obtain support services.
Under the Agreement, NSB is obligated to provide Client Desk Top Computers with the required Microsoft Windows Operating System and Terminal Services.
The Agreement allows either party to terminate at the expiration of the Initial Term by giving the other party at least 180 days’ prior written notice.

Under the Agreement, if Client exercises its early termination right after the third year of the Initial Term, the total early termination penalty payable to NSB equals one hundred percent (100%) of the then-current annual SaaS fees, calculated by combining the fifty percent (50%) penalty for each of the two remaining years of the Initial Term.

VERIFIED CLAUSE: The Agreement explicitly states that the SaaS Fees may be increased each year after the second year of the Initial Term by up to the greater of either (i) the increase in the Consumer Price Index (All Items, Montreal) over the previous year ('CPI') plus two percent (2%), or (ii) five percent (5%).

Under Section 3.3, consistent with the common-law cure doctrine, the Agreement gives the defaulting party a mandatory thirty (30) business-day cure period before termination can become effective for a material breach.

Reading Section 3.1 and Section 3.3 together, if Client terminates for NSB's material breach after the third year of the Initial Term, Client is expressly relieved of the fifty percent (50%) early-termination fee that would otherwise apply, because the termination is attributable to NSB's default rather than Client's convenience.

**THIRD AMENDMENT TO EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT**
THIS THIRD AME

attempts.claim: Because the amendment states that the Employee’s base salary and asset growth bonus will each be revisited when the Company’s assets reach $500,000,000, the agreement requires the Company to automatically renegotiate and increase both forms of compensation once that asset threshold is reached.

The agreement defines 'base salary' to include both the monthly compensation and the $1,100 vehicle allowance, such that the 6% annual increases applied to the base salary figure each January 1st also cause a corresponding 6% annual increase in the vehicle allowance amount.
The net profit bonus is payable annually on the same date each year, specifically upon completion of the audit and filing of the Form 10-K.

The total maximum compensation the Employee can receive in a single year under this agreement is capped at 300% of his base salary, combining the asset growth bonus and the net profit bonus which alone is capped at 200% of base salary.
VERIFIED CLAUSE: The Employee's vehicle allowance of $1,100 per month is payable on the first day of each month, retroactive to January 1, 2013.

Under the U.S. GAAP doctrine of consistency incorporated by Section 3(b), the amendment requires that any asset growth bonus triggered in a given year be paid only after the Company completes its annual audit and files its Form 10-K, just like the net profit bonus.

Under the combined effect of Sections 3(a) and 3(b), the Employee's vehicle allowance of $1,100 per month is subject to the same 6% annual increase applied to the base salary on each January 1st from 2013 through 2017.
Under the Third Amendment, the term 'Company's assets' as used in Section 3(b) is defined in the Employment Agreement to include only assets held by the Company's Oklahoma subsidiaries.
The Third Amendment provides that once the Company’s assets reach $500,000,000, the Employee’s base salary and asset growth bonus must be renegotiated and reset at that time.
The Third Amendment entitles the Employee to receive a net profit bonus of 5% of the Company's net income each year, with no cap on the total bonus amount payable.

The Third Amendment specifies that the Employee's total annual compensation, including base salary, vehicle allowance, asset growth bonuses, and net profit bonus, cannot exceed $1,002,932 in any year prior to the Company's assets reaching $500,000,000.

attempts.claim: Because Section 5(D) gives Licensee any recoveries from actions against third parties involved in the Container Vessel Business and Section 3(B) requires Licensee to pay the costs of trademark applications, the agreement obligates Licensor to reimburse Licensee for all enforcement and registration expenses before retaining any portion of those recoveries.

Under the agreement, the defined term 'Territory' covers only those jurisdictions where Licensee can demonstrate a legitimate business need for trademark registration, as the worldwide scope is expressly conditioned on Licensor's approval of each jurisdiction pursuant to Section 3(B).
The agreement grants the Licensee an exclusive license to use the Trademarks in the Container Vessel Business, thereby preventing the Licensor from using the Trademarks in that same business.

Under the agreement, Licensee has a combined cure period of 105 days following termination—consisting of the 90-day period to change its corporate name under Section 1(D) plus the 15-business-day quality control review period under Section 4(A)—before it must fully cease all use of the Trademarks.
VERIFIED CLAUSE: Upon termination, Licensee must immediately cease all use of the Trademarks, including removal from corporate names, within 30 days as per Section 8.

Under the naked-license doctrine reflected in UCC § 2-306’s good-faith standards, Section 4(A) requires Licensor to respond to any proposed new trademark prototype within fifteen (15) calendar days or the use is automatically deemed approved.

Under the combined effect of Sections 3(B) and 5(D), if Licensor elects not to pursue a third-party infringer after being notified by Licensee, Licensee is automatically entitled to commence an enforcement action without seeking any further authorization from Licensor, with all resulting recoveries belonging exclusively to Licensee.

Under Section 1(C), the Licensee is permitted to incorporate the Trademarks into domain names that include the Trademarks combined with any descriptive words related to its business, as long as such domain names are registered in the name of the Licensor.

Section 3(B) effectively requires the Licensee to bear all costs of trademark protection in the Territory, including the expense of maintaining and renewing existing trademark registrations used in the Container Vessel Business.

results.document_preview: **Exhibit 10.82** **JOINT AND SEVERAL HAZARDOUS MATERIAL _GUARANTY AND INDEMNIFI

attempts.claim: Because paragraph 6 excludes separate accounts maintained exclusively for payroll or taxes from the Lender’s lien and set-off rights, and paragraph 9 waives notices and demands, the agreement affirmatively requires the Lender to give prior written notice before exercising any set-off against all other accounts of the undersigned.

Under the agreement's definition of 'Hazardous Material,' petroleum products are only covered if they are also independently classified as toxic, explosive, corrosive, flammable, infectious, radioactive, carcinogenic, or mutagenic under an Environmental Requirement, meaning pure petroleum alone does not trigger the indemnification obligations.
The guarantor's indemnification obligations under Section 4 are limited to environmental damage claims that are filed in a court of law or formally asserted by a Governmental Authority.
Section 2 states that, to the best of the undersigned’s knowledge, no Hazardous Material is currently located at, on, in, under or about the Trust Property.

Under paragraph 5, all sums expended by the Lender must be reimbursed within five (5) days after written demand, and any unreimbursed amounts bear interest at the default interest rate set forth in the Note, creating a combined reimbursement obligation covering both paragraphs 4 and 5 indemnities.

VERIFIED CLAUSE: Under paragraph 6, the Lender's right of set-off applies to all accounts of the undersigned held by the Lender, including separate accounts maintained exclusively for payroll or taxes.

Pursuant to UCC § 9-604 and the common-law doctrine of marshalling, paragraph 8 authorizes the Lender, after any default, to apply all available moneys first to environmental indemnity liabilities before reducing principal or other Debt in whatever order the Lender deems fit.

Reading paragraphs 5 and 6 together, the agreement provides that any sums expended by the Lender in connection with enforcement of the Guaranty and Indemnification Agreement that are not reimbursed within five days of written demand are automatically charged against the undersigned's deposit accounts and securities held by the Lender without any further notice or demand, and such set-off amounts shall themselves bear interest at the default rate specified in the Note.

The Guarantor's indemnification obligations under paragraph 4 for personal injury or property damage arising from Hazardous Material are expressly limited to injuries occurring on the Trust Property itself.

The agreement requires the Lender to provide the undersigned with written notice and a five-day opportunity to cure before exercising any right of set-off against the undersigned’s accounts or property.

The agreement expressly provides that all sums expended by the Lender under the indemnification provisions shall bear interest at the default interest rate set forth in the Note from the date such sums are expended, regardless of whether the undersigned has received written demand for reimbursement.
The agreement caps the total liability of the undersigned for all indemnified costs and expenses at five times the default interest rate specified in the Note.

results.document_preview: **Exhibit 10.5** **THIS SECURITY AGREEMENT AMENDMENT AND THE RIGHTS PROVIDED HER

attempts.claim: Because Section 2(a) adds Sand Hill Finance, LLC’s security interests to the definition of “Permitted Encumbrances” and Section 5 states that the October 2008 Notes rank pari passu with the May 2007, November 2007, and July 2008 Notes, the amendment provides that Sand Hill Finance, LLC’s security interests also rank pari passu with all of the Secured Parties’ security interests.

The amended definition of 'Notes' in Section 2(b) expressly includes the October 2008 Notes within the scope of the original Security Agreement dated May 29, 2007, thereby making the October 2008 Notes subject to all of the same covenants, representations, and warranties that applied to the May 2007 Notes at the time of their original issuance.
The amendment requires that any future amendments to the Security Agreement must be consented to by all Secured Parties, including the October 2008 Secured Parties, before becoming effective.

The aggregate principal amount of all promissory notes issued by AXS-One Inc. across the May 2007, November 2007, July 2008, and October 2008 financings totals Eleven Million Nine Hundred and Fifty Thousand Dollars ($11,950,000), representing the total secured obligations under the Security Agreement as amended.

VERIFIED CLAUSE: The Third Security Agreement Amendment amends Section 1(c) of the Security Agreement to include Sand Hill Finance, LLC's security interests as a Permitted Encumbrance only if such interests are subordinate to the Secured Parties' security interests as established in the Second Amended and Restated Subordination Agreement dated October 30, 2008.

Under New York law and UCC § 9-339, as reflected in Section 2(a), the amendment expressly confirms that Sand Hill Finance, LLC’s security interests are contractually subordinated to the Secured Parties and therefore qualify as a Permitted Encumbrance only on a junior-lien basis pursuant to the Second Amended and Restated Subordination Agreement dated October 30, 2008.

Under Section 5 of the Amendment, the Secured Parties' consent to pari passu ranking expressly extends to Sand Hill Finance, LLC's security interests, such that Sand Hill Finance, LLC's liens are confirmed to rank equally in priority with the May 2007 Notes, November 2007 Notes, July 2008 Notes, and October 2008 Notes for all purposes under the Security Agreement.
Under Section 2(b), the defined term 'Secured Parties' includes specifically only the holders of the October 2008 Notes that are made party to the Security Agreement pursuant to this Third Amendment.

Section 5 provides that all principal and interest payments on the October 2008 Notes must be made pro rata with the May 2007, November 2007, and July 2008 Notes because the parties agreed the liens and payment rights would rank pari passu.

Section 5 of the Amendment expressly provides that the October 2008 Notes rank pari passu with the May 2007 Notes, November 2007 Notes, and July 2008 Notes in priority of payment and in all other respects, and that this pari passu ranking applies unconditionally regardless of the order in which the respective notes were issued or the security interests were granted.
The total principal amount secured by this Amendment across all notes (May 2007, November 2007, July 2008, and October 2008) is $12,850,000.

Under Sections 4 and 6, after the Proposed Transactions the Special Limited Partner’s election to receive Net Sales Proceeds instead of exchanging and redeeming its Special Partnership Units is irrevocable and may not be amended or reassigned without the General Partner’s prior written consent.

Under this Agreement, the term 'Proposed Transactions' encompasses all merger, asset transfer, and any future sale transactions that Prologis, L.P. may undertake following the closing of the merger, as the definition expressly includes transactions contemplated by the Merger Agreement and any subsequent dispositions of assets acquired thereunder.

Under this Agreement, the Special Limited Partner holds a combined total of two distinct classes of partnership interests in Operating Partnership — 100% of the Special Partnership Units and 100% of the Class A Units — giving it majority economic control over distributions under Section 5.2(b)(i) of the Limited Partnership Agreement.

VERIFIED CLAUSE: Section 4 explicitly states that the Special Limited Partner retains the unilateral right to demand immediate cash redemption of all Special Partnership Units through the Conversion and Redemption Provision following any future Sale, Termination Event, or Liquidity Event, regardless of the General Partner's consent.

Section 6 expressly adopts Delaware law, and under the Delaware parol evidence rule and Article 11 amendment formalities, that means this side agreement supersedes any contrary understanding and permanently waives Section 8.7’s Conversion and Redemption Provision for all future Sale, Termination Event, and Liquidity Event scenarios, not just the Proposed Transactions.

Under Section 4, following the Proposed Transactions the Special Limited Partner's discretion to choose between redemption of Special Partnership Units or cash distribution of Net Sales Proceeds is irrevocable once exercised, as Section 6 provides that no amendment is valid without a signed writing from both parties, meaning the Special Limited Partner cannot reverse or modify its election without the General Partner's written consent.

Under Section 4, the Special Limited Partner may choose to receive distributions of Net Sales Proceeds in accordance with Section 5.2(b)(i)(B)(2) of the Limited Partnership Agreement for any Sale, Termination Event or Liquidity Event, including the Proposed Transactions themselves.
Section 6 permits either party to assign this side agreement to an affiliate or successor without obtaining the other party’s prior written consent.

Under Section 4, following the Proposed Transactions, the Special Limited Partner is entitled to receive distributions of Net Sales Proceeds in accordance with Section 5.2(b)(i)(B)(2) of the Limited Partnership Agreement as the default outcome of any future Sale, Termination Event, or Liquidity Event.

The contract caps the total distributions to the Special Limited Partner from any future Sale, Termination Event, or Liquidity Event at the amount determined under Section 5.2(b)(i)(B)(2) of the Limited Partnership Agreement.

attempts.claim: Because Section 3.16 requires the Company to cause the Trustee to pay the cash portion of the Deferred Underwriting Commission directly from the Trust Account upon consummation of the initial Business Combination, and Section III(f) confirms Cantor holds 200,000 Specified Shares, the amendment guarantees Cantor will receive an additional $2,000,000 in cash from the Trust Account at the Acquisition Closing Date in lieu of any Common Stock otherwise payable under clauses (ii) and (iii).

Under the Amendment's definition of 'Third Party Private Investors,' any investment funds affiliated with or managed by Cantor Fitzgerald & Co. are expressly excluded from that term, meaning proceeds from Cantor-affiliated equity investments would not count toward the Third Party Equity Value calculation.

Because Section 2 excludes Silicon Valley Bank or its permitted transferee from making a demand registration request, and Section 1(e)(3) includes Common Stock issuable upon conversion of Silicon Valley Bank’s Series E warrants within Registrable Securities, Silicon Valley Bank may hold Registrable Securities but is barred from both initiating a Section 2 demand registration and participating in any registration under this Agreement.

Under Section 1(e), 'Registrable Securities' expressly includes the Common Stock issuable upon exercise (not merely conversion) of the Common Stock Purchase Warrants held by the Warrant Holders listed on Schedule 3, as the definition specifically enumerates warrant exercise proceeds alongside preferred stock conversion shares.
Under Section: 'Corporation Registration', the Corporation must provide Holders at least 60 days' written notice before filing any registration statement for its own account.

Under this Agreement, the Corporation's combined deferral and postponement rights under Section 2 allow it to delay a requested registration for a total maximum period of up to six (6) months in any twelve-month period, calculated by adding the three-month good-faith deferral period under clause (b) and the three-month audit postponement period under clause (c).

VERIFIED CLAUSE: Under Section 2, the Corporation may postpone a registration request for up to three (3) months based on a President's certificate of detrimental effect, and this deferral right can be exercised repeatedly without annual limit.

Under Section 2, because Silicon Valley Bank is expressly excluded from initiating a demand registration request, any Registrable Securities held by Silicon Valley Bank are nonetheless counted toward the fifty percent (50%) threshold calculation needed for other Holders to trigger a valid demand registration.
Under Section 3, Holders of Registrable Securities have the right to request inclusion of their securities in any Corporation registration, including those filed on Forms S-4 or S-8.

Because Section 2 allows a written demand only after six months following the Corporation’s first public offering, the Agreement does not grant any piggyback registration rights before the IPO and all registration rights become effective only after that six-month post-IPO period.

results.document_preview: * * * _STRICTLY CONFIDENTIAL_ Execution Version ESCROW AGREEMENT This Escrow Agr

attempts.claim: Because ATI is treated as the owner of the Escrow Property for tax purposes and the Escrow Agent holds the funds in its name as agent for ATI, ATI may unilaterally revise the escrow account investment instructions without needing the Purchaser Parties’ consent.

Under this Escrow Agreement, the 'Escrow Release Instruction' is defined as a written instruction that must be jointly delivered by both ATI and the Purchaser Parties to the Escrow Agent in order to authorize any disbursement of the Escrow Property.
Under this Escrow Agreement, the Escrow Agent is required to invest the Escrow Property in interest-bearing accounts to generate income for the benefit of the Parties.
ATI may instruct the Escrow Agent to disburse the full $6,000,000 of Escrow Property to ATI once the Tender Offer Conditions are satisfied or waived and ATI agrees to accept the Shares for purchase.

The Escrow Agreement provides that if the Tender Offer Conditions are not satisfied or waived by February 28, 2025, ATI must deliver an Escrow Release Instruction within 5 business days of that deadline, releasing the full $6,000,000 to the Purchaser Parties.
VERIFIED CLAUSE: The Escrow Agent is required to invest the Escrow Property in interest-bearing accounts, with all interest earned to be distributed to ATI as the owner of the funds.

Pursuant to UCC Article 4A’s commercially reasonable security procedure standard, Section 1.4 requires the Escrow Agent to obtain dual authorization from ATI and at least one Purchaser Party before executing any funds transfer from the Escrow Account.

Under the combined operation of Sections 1.2(a) and 1.5(a), the Escrow Agent is required to hold the Escrow Property in a segregated, interest-bearing account in ATI's name, with all interest and earnings accruing to ATI as the tax owner of the Escrow Property.

Under Section 1.3(a), the Required Amount is defined as the portion of the Escrow Property necessary for the Permitted Use of Third Amendment Note Proceeds, which is expressly set at $6,000,000 in the Escrow Agreement.

The Escrow Agreement requires ATI to commence the Tender Offer no later than the Pre-Funding Date, since the Purchaser Parties must fund the full $6,000,000 into escrow on or before that date in anticipation of the offer.
Under Section 1.2, the Escrow Agent is directed to hold the Escrow Property in one or more segregated, interest-bearing bank accounts maintained in its name as agent for ATI.

The total amount that Purchaser Parties must deliver to the Escrow Agent under Section 1.1 is $6,000,000, but if the Required Amount for the Permitted Use of Third Amendment Note Proceeds is less than $6,000,000, ATI can only receive up to $3,000,000 from the escrow.

The amendment provides that the up to $20,000,000 Restricted Payment to the Sponsors to fund Acquisition III LLC’s purchase of the Managing GP may be treated as an Investment permitted under Section 6.7(r).

Under the amended definition of 'Related Agreements,' the term expressly encompasses the Swap Agreement, the Management Agreement, the Partnership Agreement, and all intercompany agreements among the Managing GP, the MLP, the Company, Acquisition III LLC, or CVR or any of their subsidiaries.
The MLP Reorganization must be completed on or before August 23, 2007, as the Partnership Agreement is required to be dated on that date.

The aggregate cap on Restricted Payments to the Sponsors under the Amendment is $20,000,000, combining both the payment to fund Acquisition III LLC's purchase of the Managing GP and the net proceeds from the Managing GP sale that must be applied to outstanding Loans.
VERIFIED CLAUSE: The Amendment explicitly prohibits the MLP and Special GP from incurring any debt, liens, or other financial obligations without prior written consent from all Lenders.

Under UCC Article 9’s after-acquired collateral doctrine, the Amendment’s addition of MergerSub 1 and MergerSub 2 as Grantors automatically means all of their present and future assets became perfected collateral for the Secured Parties as of the First Amendment Effective Date without any further pledge, security agreement, or filing being required.

The Amendment expressly requires that the net proceeds from the sale of the Managing GP to Acquisition III LLC must be applied in accordance with Section 2.14(a) AND that such sale price must equal at least $20,000,000, because the GP Purchase Price is defined as the amount of the Restricted Payment actually paid to the Sponsors, which Section 6.5(a)(x) caps at $20,000,000.

The Amendment defines 'MLP Reorganization' to include the formation of the Managing GP, the MLP, and the Special GP by the Company, the contribution of assets to the MLP, the sale of Managing GP Capital Stock to Acquisition III LLC, and the Restricted Payment to the Sponsors, all of which must be completed simultaneously on the First Amendment Effective Date.

The Amendment unconditionally entitles all Lenders who execute the Amendment to an amendment fee equal to 0.05% of their outstanding Loans and Commitments on the First Amendment Effective Date, regardless of when they execute.
The Amendment sets the aggregate cap on Consolidated Capital Expenditures for Fiscal Years 2008 through 2011 at $380,000,000.

The Amendment provides that, once the Second Amendment Effective Date occurs, the revised Applicable Margin table applies retroactively to all B Term Loans outstanding from June 28, 2002 under the Credit Agreement, and all references in the Credit Documents are deemed to incorporate that retroactive repricing.

Under the Amendment, the defined term 'Second Amendment Effective Date' means July 15, 2004, the date expressly stated in the Amendment's opening recital as the date on which the Amendment was executed and delivered.

The Amendment requires that the Borrowers deliver a signed counterpart of the Amendment to the Administrative Agent via telecopier no later than the date on which the Second Amendment is dated, July 15, 2004, for the Amendment to become effective.

attempts.claim: Because Novavax controls recalls and pharmacovigilance under Section 2.7 and delivery obligations are subject to SIIPL/SLS obtaining all Regulatory Approvals under Section 2.8, the Supply Agreement expressly makes Novavax solely responsible for securing all export permissions and other governmental approvals required to ship Product to the Novavax Exclusive Territory and SIIPL Non-Exclusive Territory.

Under Section 1.5, 'Commercialization' as defined in the Supply Agreement expressly includes Medical Affairs activities undertaken by Novavax in the Novavax Exclusive Territory or SIIPL Non-Exclusive Territory, provided those activities are directed to the marketing or promotion of the Product.
Under Section 2.6, Novavax has the right to reject any non-conforming batch of Product and require SIIPL to replace it at no additional cost to Novavax.

Under Articles 1 and 2, the Supply Agreement requires Novavax to provide a Statement of Work at least 30 days before issuing a Proposed Purchase Order, and SIIPL/SLS must then respond to that Proposed Purchase Order within 30 days of receipt, meaning the combined minimum lead time from SOW submission to a binding Firm Purchase Order is at least 60 days.

VERIFIED CLAUSE: Under Section 2.4.1, SIIPL/SLS is solely responsible for arranging and paying for all shipping, insurance, and customs clearance costs to deliver the Product to the Product Delivery Destination.

Pursuant to UCC § 2-207 and expressly reflected in Section 2.2.2, any additional or different terms included in Novavax’s Firm Purchase Order automatically become part of this Supply Agreement unless SIIPL or SLS objects to them in writing within a reasonable time.

Under this Supply Agreement, SIIPL and SLS are jointly required to notify Novavax of acceptance, rejection, or proposed modification of a Proposed Purchase Order within the same timeframe that SIIPL must also confirm availability of sufficient Adjuvant quantities, since Section 2.2.1 ties the rejection right directly to Novavax's ability to provide the necessary Adjuvant.
Under the Supply Agreement, 'Commercialization' includes Medical Affairs activities directed towards the Product in the Novavax Exclusive Territory or SIIPL Non-Exclusive Territory.
The Supply Agreement requires Novavax to provide SIIPL and SLS with a non-binding Statement of Work at least 30 days before issuing any Proposed Purchase Order.

Under the Supply Agreement, neither SIIPL nor SLS may reject any Proposed Purchase Order that sets forth the same quantities and delivery dates as the applicable SOW, regardless of whether Novavax is able to provide the necessary quantities of Adjuvant required for such volumes.

Under the Supply Agreement, the total maximum quantity of Product that Novavax may order across all Firm Purchase Orders in any calendar year is capped at the sum of the quantities specified in all Statements of Work executed during that year.

attempts.claim: Because the license is exclusive in the Territory and Field and remains in effect until the earlier of termination of the JV Contract or Licensor’s exercise of the put option, Licensor is prohibited during that entire period from using or licensing the Licensed Trademarks to any other party in the PRC, Hong Kong, Macau, or Taiwan for Licensed Products.

Under this Trademark License Agreement, the 'Territory' is defined to include the PRC, Hong Kong S.A.R., Macau S.A.R., Taiwan, and any other jurisdiction in which Licensor markets or sells Licensed Products during the Term, as Schedule B may be updated to reflect such additional markets.
Under this Trademark License Agreement, Licensee must obtain Licensor's prior written approval for any advertising or promotional materials that use the Licensed Trademarks.

Under this Trademark License Agreement, the cure period for a material breach is a combined maximum of 60 days — comprising the initial 30-day cure period plus an additional 30-day extension — before the non-defaulting Party may terminate the agreement.

VERIFIED CLAUSE: Under Section 6.3(b) of the Trademark License Agreement, termination for a material breach is permissible only after a 30-day cure period, and if the breach persists beyond that period, the non-defaulting Party must then provide a second written notice and allow an additional 30-day extension before termination.

Consistent with the Lanham Act’s quality-control doctrine and expressly reflected in Section 2.2, the Agreement requires Licensee to submit all advertising and promotional materials bearing the Licensed Trademarks to Licensor for prior written approval before any public use in the Territory.

Under Section 2.1 and Section 6.3(b) read together, if Licensee fails to cure a material breach within 30 days of written notice, the license granted under this Agreement automatically converts from exclusive to non-exclusive for the remainder of the Term rather than terminating outright.
Under Section 1.14, 'Territory' includes only the PRC and explicitly excludes Hong Kong, Macau, and Taiwan.

Because the license granted in Section 2.1 is exclusive in the Territory and Field, Licensor is prohibited during the Term from using the Licensed Trademarks itself in the Territory for the Licensed Products.
Section 6.3(b) provides that either Party may terminate the Agreement for the other Party's material breach immediately upon written notice, without any cure period.
The total cure period for any material breach under Section 6.3(b) is capped at 30 days, regardless of any mutual agreement to extend it.

If the Executive resigns for Good Reason within 24 months after a Change in Control, Middlefield must both pay the severance within five business days and continue the Executive’s life, health, and disability coverage for a full 24 months regardless of whether the Executive becomes employed by another employer.
Under this Severance Agreement, the term 'subsidiary' is defined to include any entity in which Middlefield directly or indirectly beneficially owns 25% or more of the outstanding voting securities.
If the Executive voluntarily terminates employment for any reason during the first 12 months after a Change in Control, the Executive is entitled to the lump-sum severance payment under section 1(a).

Upon a termination for Good Reason within 24 months after a Change in Control, Middlefield must continue the Executive’s life, health, and disability insurance coverage for a full 24 months after termination.

Under this Severance Agreement, the maximum total cash severance benefit payable to the Executive—combining the two-times annual compensation lump sum under section 1(a) with the present value of 24 months of life, health, and disability insurance coverage under section 1(c)—is expressly capped at three times the Executive's annual compensation.

VERIFIED CLAUSE: Under section 1(c), if Middlefield elects to provide a cash payment in lieu of continued insurance coverage, the amount added to the lump sum is explicitly defined as the present value of the projected cost for 18 months of coverage.

Under the federal golden parachute rules referenced in the recitals—12 U.S.C. 1828(k)(4)(A)(ii) and 12 C.F.R. 359.1(f)(1)(ii)—the agreement expressly makes all severance benefits contingent on prior FDIC approval if Middlefield later becomes troubled, notwithstanding section 1’s payment terms.

Under the combined operation of sections 1(a) and 1(c), if Middlefield elects to pay the present-value cash equivalent in lieu of continued insurance coverage, that additional cash amount is itself subject to the two-times multiplier applied to annual compensation, effectively tripling the Executive's potential lump-sum severance.

Under the definition of 'Change in Control' in section 2(b), the acquisition of 25% or more of Middlefield's voting securities by any person, including a subsidiary holding shares in a fiduciary capacity, triggers a Change in Control.

Section 1(c) effectively guarantees the Executive a full 24 months of continued life, health, and disability insurance coverage after a qualifying termination, even if the Executive becomes employed by another employer during that period.

Section 1(a) provides that the lump-sum severance payment equal to two times the Executive's annual compensation is payable no later than five business days after the date of employment termination, and this payment shall not be reduced, discounted to present value, or subject to any withholding or offset for amounts owed by the Executive to Middlefield.

Section 1(c) states that if Middlefield elects to pay a cash equivalent for continued life, health, and disability coverage instead of providing the coverage, the total lump-sum payment under Section 1(a) must be increased by the present value of the cost for 36 months of coverage.
